theorem marginalize_extendingCDMGsWith_comm (G : CDMG Node)
    (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node) (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V)
    (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.extendingCDMGsWith W₁ hW₁).marginalize (W₂.image IntExtNode.unsplit)
        (image_unsplit_subset_extendingCDMGsWith_V hW₁ hW₂)
      = (G.marginalize W₂ hW₂).extendingCDMGsWith W₁
        (subset_carrier_of_marginalize hW₂ hW₁ hDisj)
lean_blocks.explanation: This is Part (ii) of the row: marginalization commutes with the intervention-node extension $G_{\doit(I_{W_1})}$ as a literal `=` of CDMGs over the extended carrier `CDMG (IntExtNode Node)`. The carrier matches on both sides because `extendingCDMGsWith` is the operator that introduces the fresh intervention symbols $I_w$ (modeled as the `IntExtNode` sum type), and marginalization preserves whichever carrier it acts on. The asymmetry between the two sides is the LHS's marginalization set: it must be a `Finset (IntExtNode Node)`, supplied as `W₂.image IntExtNode.unsplit` — the canonical lift of the original-carrier set into its embedded slice. The subset obligation on this lift is discharged by `image_unsplit_subset_extendingCDMGsWith_V hW₁ hW₂`, while the RHS's outer `extendingCDMGsWith W₁` reuses Part (i)'s `subset_carrier_of_marginalize hW₂ hW₁ hDisj`. The signature reads identically to Part (i) on the parameter side (same typing on `W₁`, `W₂` and the same `Disjoint` binder), so the three theorems can be cited in parallel; the difference is purely in the operators applied and the carrier that results.

-- Part (ii): marginalization commutes with adding intervention nodes; both sides over `IntExtNode Node`.
theorem marginalize_extendingCDMGsWith_comm (G : CDMG Node)
    (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node) (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V)
    (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    -- LHS marginalizes over `W₂.image .unsplit`, the canonical lift into the extended carrier.
    (G.extendingCDMGsWith W₁ hW₁).marginalize (W₂.image IntExtNode.unsplit)
        (image_unsplit_subset_extendingCDMGsWith_V hW₁ hW₂)
    -- RHS extends after marginalizing; same carrier-transport helper as Part (i).
      = (G.marginalize W₂ hW₂).extendingCDMGsWith W₁
        (subset_carrier_of_marginalize hW₂ hW₁ hDisj)

theorem marginalize_nodeSplittingOn_comm (G : CDMG Node)
    (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node) (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V)
    (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W₁ hW₁).marginalize (W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit)
        (image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_disjoint hW₁ hW₂ hDisj.symm)
      = (G.marginalize W₂ hW₂).nodeSplittingOn W₁
        (subset_sdiff_of_disjoint hW₁ hDisj)
lean_blocks.explanation: This is Part (iii) of the row: marginalization commutes with node-splitting $G_{\spl(W_1)}$ as a literal `=` of CDMGs over `CDMG (SplitNode Node)`. The departure from Parts (i) and (ii) is the typing on `W₁`: it tightens from `W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` to `W₁ ⊆ G.V`, because `nodeSplittingOn` (`def_3_11`) removes `W₁` from the output nodes and replaces it with tagged copies $w^0$, $w^1$ — an operation that only makes sense on output nodes. The LHS lifts `W₂` into the split carrier via `W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit`, with the subset obligation discharged by `image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_disjoint hW₁ hW₂ hDisj.symm` — the only helper in this file that consumes disjointness, because the split carrier's `V` has three pieces (`(G.V \ W₁).image .unsplit`, `W₁.image .copy0`, `W₁.image .copy1`) and the lift must land in the leftmost. The RHS reuses Part (i)'s `subset_sdiff_of_disjoint hW₁ hDisj` for the outer `nodeSplittingOn`'s `W₁ ⊆ G.V \ W₂` obligation. Together the three theorems exhaust the LN's enumerated intervention operators, each commuting with marginalization in its own native carrier.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Part (iii): marginalization commutes with node-splitting; carrier is `CDMG (SplitNode Node)`.
theorem marginalize_nodeSplittingOn_comm (G : CDMG Node)
    -- Tightened typing `W₁ ⊆ G.V` (no `∪ G.J`) — `nodeSplittingOn` only acts on output nodes.
    (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node) (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V)
    (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    -- LHS marginalizes over `W₂.image .unsplit`; disjointness routes the lift into the `.unsplit` slice.
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W₁ hW₁).marginalize (W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit)
        (image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_disjoint hW₁ hW₂ hDisj.symm)
    -- RHS splits after marginalizing; outer `nodeSplittingOn` needs `W₁ ⊆ G.V \ W₂`.
      = (G.marginalize W₂ hW₂).nodeSplittingOn W₁
        (subset_sdiff_of_disjoint hW₁ hDisj)

addition_to_the_LN: [trailing_similar_statement_two_unstated_claims] This lemma comprises three distinct claims, all of which must be formalized: (1) the explicit equation $(G_{\doit(W_1)})^{\sm W_2} = (G^{\sm W_2})_{\doit(W_1)}$ stated above; (2) the analogous commutativity of marginalization with adding intervention nodes; and (3) the analogous commutativity of marginalization with node-splitting interventions. Claims (2) and (3) are to be stated with analogous typing and disjointness side conditions on $W_1, W_2$, with the hard-intervention operator $\doit(W_1)$ replaced by the corresponding intervention operation in each case.
design_choices:
**Three theorems, not one parametric statement.** The LN bundles the three parts under a single `\begin{Lem}` heading and a closing "a similar statement holds" clause, but the three return-carriers differ — `CDMG Node`, `CDMG (IntExtNode Node)`, `CDMG (SplitNode Node)`. Lean has no natural way to host a parametric quantification over "intervention operator" that lets the return type vary with the operator, so the three-theorem shape mirrors the LN's enumeration literally. The split also matters for downstream citation: later chapters cite Part (i) alone (e.g. in the ID-algorithm manipulations $G_{\doit(C)}^{\sm B}$ and in counterfactual constructions), and bundling would obstruct selective reference.

**One Lean file, not three.** The parts share the same upstream-def chain (`def_3_1`, `def_3_10`/`_11`/`_13`, `def_3_14`), the same template helpers, and the same proof strategy (CDMG field-by-field extensionality on `J`, `V`, `E`, `L`). Splitting per-part would scatter the lift helpers across three files and force triple re-import of the same chain.

**Literal `=` of CDMGs, not an equality-up-to-isomorphism.** For each part both sides of the displayed equation are CDMGs of the same Lean type, because the operators in each part preserve a common carrier (`hardInterventionOn` preserves `Node`; `extendingCDMGsWith` and the outer `extendingCDMGsWith` both produce `CDMG (IntExtNode Node)`; analogously for node-splitting). No `eqViaNodeMap` workaround is needed.

**Inner-marginalize carrier lift via `Finset.image .unsplit`.** In Parts (ii) and (iii), the inner CDMG already lives over the extended carrier, so the outer marginalization must consume a `Finset` of the extended carrier. The natural lift of $W_2 : \mathrm{Finset}\ \mathrm{Node}$ is `W₂.image IntExtNode.unsplit` (resp. `SplitNode.unsplit`), which lands in the `.unsplit`-image slice of the extended `V`. The LN-faithful reading is that marginalization is applied to the *original* output nodes, never to fresh intervention symbols or tagged copies — and by `Disjoint W₁ W₂`, none of `W₂`'s elements has a `.copy0`/`.copy1` companion in the extended graph anyway.

**Disjointness binder oriented as `Disjoint W₁ W₂`.** This matches the LN's symmetric phrasing $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ and aligns with the sibling rows (`claim_3_4`, `claim_3_8`, `claim_3_17`). The carrier-transport helpers consume specific orientations: where the helper takes `Disjoint W₂ W₁`, the call site composes with `.symm`. Keeping the binder in LN orientation means the theorem signature reads naturally; the orientation flips are confined to the helper-call arguments.

**Typing departure in Part (iii).** Parts (i) and (ii) take `W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` (matching the preconditions of `hardInterventionOn` and `extendingCDMGsWith`), but Part (iii) tightens to `W₁ ⊆ G.V`. This is forced by `nodeSplittingOn` (`def_3_11`), which removes `W₁` from `V` and creates tagged copies — an operation that only makes sense on output nodes. The rewritten canonical tex statement reflects this typing distinction explicitly.

**Field-by-field equality deferred to the proof.** Equality of two CDMGs unfolds via the structure injectivity inherited from `def_3_1` to a conjunction on the four data fields `J`, `V`, `E`, `L` (the five propositional fields are discharged by proof irrelevance). The statements do not bake this unpacking in; each proof opens with a local `cdmgExt` extensionality lemma and then dispatches the four field equalities, matching the rewritten tex's closing remark.
